skill.name: lottie
skill.description: Guides HyperFrames projects that embed lottie-web JSON animations and dotLottie players with deterministic seeking, local assets, stable sizing, and shared registration.
skill.summary: Embed and validate Lottie or dotLottie animations in HyperFrames compositions.
skill.author: heygen-com

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command patterns, but these are Markdown backticks and fenced examples, not executable Ruby or shell code. The real issues are low-risk documentation patterns: example CDN script URLs and optional npx validation commands, with no evidence of malicious intent or data exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Remote CDN Script Examples
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The examples load lottie-web and dotLottie players from public CDNs. This is a supply-chain and availability concern if copied directly, but it is normal documentation and shows no malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are direct script sources in HTML examples. Risk is limited because the file is documentation and does not execute by itself.

low_findings.title: Optional External CLI Validation Commands
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE: The skill suggests running npx hyperframes lint and npx hyperframes validate. These are expected validation commands, but users should run them only in trusted projects.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible in a bash validation block. They are legitimate project checks, with no dynamic arguments or hidden payloads.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick External Command Flags
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown inline code and fenced examples. They do not indicate Ruby backtick execution or command interpolation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is Markdown prose and example code blocks. No Ruby source file, shell interpolation, or executable wrapper is present.

low_findings.title: Documentation Reference URLs
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs to GitHub and LottieFiles documentation are references, not network calls. No evidence found of automatic requests or data transfer.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs appear under Credits And References. They are not embedded scripts and do not run during skill use.

low_findings.title: Static Blocker Pattern False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The weak cryptographic algorithm and system reconnaissance flags are not supported by the file context. Line 3 is descriptive metadata, and line 91 starts an Avoid section.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No cryptographic function, hash algorithm call, host inspection, or system enumeration behavior appears at these locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Public CDN Script Loading
dangerous_patterns.description: Example HTML loads animation player libraries from public CDN URLs. This should be pinned, mirrored, or reviewed before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicitly shown as script src URLs. The concern is supply-chain exposure, not confirmed malicious intent.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Validation Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The validation section recommends npx commands. This is expected for HyperFrames validation but still invokes external tooling in the user environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are documented plainly and have no obfuscation. They are still external command execution if the user runs them.

content.value_statement: HyperFrames projects need Lottie players that can be paused, sized, and seeked reliably. This skill gives repeatable patterns for lottie-web, dotLottie, and validation.
actual_capabilities: Shows how to embed lottie-web JSON animations in HyperFrames HTML.
Shows how to embed dotLottie players with a canvas target.
Registers animation instances on window.__hfLottie for synchronized seeking.
Recommends stable CSS dimensions for animation containers.
Documents validation commands for HyperFrames linting and validation.
Lists common Lottie usage limits and export checks.
limitations: Does not include executable adapter source code.
Does not validate animation assets automatically.
Depends on project-provided Lottie JSON or .lottie files.
Example CDN scripts should be reviewed before production use.

use_cases.title: Add Product Motion
use_cases.description: Embed a product walkthrough animation that HyperFrames can seek frame by frame.

use_cases.title: Port Remotion Lottie Work
use_cases.description: Translate an existing Remotion Lottie setup into plain HyperFrames HTML patterns.

use_cases.title: Validate Animation Exports
use_cases.description: Check that After Effects exports use stable assets, disabled autoplay, and predictable playback.

prompt_templates.title: Embed One Animation
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a single lottie-web asset.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the lottie skill to add one local Lottie JSON animation to my HyperFrames page. Keep autoplay disabled and register it for seeking.

prompt_templates.title: Use dotLottie Canvas
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a HyperFrames dotLottie canvas pattern for assets/product-flow.lottie. Include stable sizing and register the player on window.__hfLottie.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Multiple Players
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced setup with several animation layers.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my HyperFrames page and show how to register multiple Lottie players so they seek together during composition rendering.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Export Reliability
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect this HyperFrames Lottie implementation for deterministic rendering risks, remote asset usage, unsupported effects, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: A HyperFrames page needs a logo reveal from a local Lottie JSON file.
output_examples.output: The assistant describes a stable container, disabled autoplay, local asset loading, and registration for timeline seeking.

output_examples.input: A project has a .lottie product animation that must render inside a canvas.
output_examples.output: The assistant outlines a dotLottie canvas setup with local source usage, disabled looping, and shared player registration.

output_examples.input: A team wants to check whether an After Effects export is ready for HyperFrames.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists validation checks for unsupported effects, remote paths, late registration, and HyperFrames linting.

best_practices: Use local animation assets under the project assets directory when possible.
Disable autoplay and looping unless the composition explicitly requires looping.
Register every player before HyperFrames validation inspects the page.
anti_patterns: Do not rely on remote animation paths during render time.
Do not start animations with play when deterministic seeking is required.
Do not assume every After Effects effect exports correctly to Lottie.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps add lottie-web and dotLottie animations to HyperFrames pages with predictable seeking behavior.

faq.question: Does it include animation files?
faq.answer: No. You provide the Lottie JSON or .lottie assets from your own project.

faq.question: Can it use remote animation URLs?
faq.answer: The guidance recommends local assets and warns against remote paths during render time.

faq.question: Does it support multiple animations?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to register several player instances in the same shared registry.

faq.question: Is it specific to HyperFrames?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance focuses on HyperFrames runtime adapter expectations and validation.

faq.question: Should CDN examples be used in production?
faq.answer: Review CDN usage first. Production projects may prefer pinned, mirrored, or locally managed dependencies.
